lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market ski-area operators providing repeat lift access and related rentals, lessons, food service, snowmaking, grooming and guest services to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Government or nonprofit facilities, captive amenities within larger lodging or real-estate businesses, shells, internal operating units, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Direct consumer revenue from day tickets and advance season passes, with repeat ancillary spending on lessons, rentals, dining and other mountain services; operations are highly seasonal and weather-dependent.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for broader NAICS 713900 rather than ski facilities, while Vail Resorts provides a large-operator workforce and cost proxy rather than LMM occupational detail. I assign high task exposure to customer service, ticketing, reservations, marketing, bookkeeping, reporting, purchasing and demand forecasting; partial exposure to staffing, rental administration, snowmaking optimization and food ordering; and low exposure to lift operations, ski patrol, instruction, grooming, maintenance and food preparation. The broad BLS population may overstate fitness and office work, and Vail's corporate scale may overstate centralized digital work, biasing exposure upward relative to an independent ski hill.
a.rationale: Ski areas have digitizable ticket, pass, rental, lesson and customer workflows, and a small independent mountain has demonstrated an integrated online system that supports snowmaking decisions. Yet the operating workforce is overwhelmingly seasonal and frontline: Vail reported about 44,900 seasonal employees versus about 7,600 year-round employees, and safety-critical physical work limits substitution. Some snowmaking is already automated and therefore is excluded from current not-yet-automated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A ski-facility-only payroll census by occupation and wage, combined with task-level time studies at independent operators covering ticketing, rentals, guest communications, snowmaking, grooming, lift operations, patrol, instruction and food service.
caveats: NAICS 713900 includes many non-ski recreation businesses.
Vail is a multinational platform with centralized functions unlike an LMM independent operator.
Digitization and rules-based automation are not necessarily AI, and already-automated tasks must not be counted.

rho.bridge: The AP case documents integrated online ticketing, lesson reservations, rentals, demand data and automated entitlement tracking at independent ski areas, but it measures adoption examples rather than the fraction of all exposed opportunity implemented across representative LMM facilities. I discount for capital scarcity, legacy lift and POS systems, seasonal implementation windows, cybersecurity, variable connectivity, staff training, and safety accountability. Showcase operators likely overstate typical adoption speed.
rho.rationale: Direct labor pressure and volatile weather make better forecasting, self-service and workload planning valuable. A five-year window is sufficient for many cloud and customer-service workflows, while mountain automation is slowed by high capital costs, narrow installation seasons, regulatory inspection and the severe consequence of errors. Software at one operator was also described as challenging and slow, demonstrating change friction.
rho.change_evidence: Repeated NSAA technology surveys reporting deployment, workflow coverage, integration cost, system downtime and realized labor hours avoided by facility size, region and independent versus multi-resort ownership.
caveats: Published examples are selected for technology adoption and may not generalize.
Safety-critical tasks require human override and formal operating procedures.
Limited capital and shoulder-season cash flow can delay implementation at smaller areas.

e.rationale: Most firms classified as skiing facilities sell a repeat external service and therefore fit the default lens. Exclusions arise from government and nonprofit operators, ski hills captive to lodging or real estate, multi-resort groups whose consolidated economics exceed the band, and entities where the operating concession cannot transfer. NSAA counted 492 operating ski areas in 2024/25 and its ownership list shows both large multi-area platforms and public or contract-operated properties, but neither source maps facilities to the frozen LMM firm population, so this remains bounded judgment.
e.change_evidence: A facility-to-firm ownership map for all 492 operating areas showing tax status, government ownership, ultimate parent, operating concession, consolidated receipts, normalized EBITDA and whether ski operations are separable from lodging or real estate.
caveats: Facility, establishment and firm counts do not align one-for-one.
The frozen LMM firm count is an estimate based on a sector margin bridge.
Management contracts and public-land permits can make operational control transferable even when land ownership is not.

s5.bridge: Census reports that over half of US employer-business owners were age 55 or older, but this is cross-industry and not a ski-owner exit rate. NSAA's ownership map and the documented Black Mountain purchase show that control transfers occur, but provide no denominator or annual frequency. I translate succession pressure and observed transactions into a five-year probability while excluding municipalizations, cooperative restructurings, internal reorganizations and property sales without continuing ski operations. Owner age likely overstates actual exits, while visible transaction examples may overrepresent larger or distressed mountains.
s5.rationale: Independent and family ski areas can face succession, capital and climate-driven sale decisions, and strategic operators actively consolidate facilities. Transfers are nevertheless hard to execute because of lifts, deferred capital, environmental exposure, land leases or public permits, snowmaking water rights and uncertain weather economics. Those frictions support a wide range around a moderate five-year probability.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year NSAA or SAM transaction census matched to the eligible firm universe and coded for seller age, buyer control, related-party status, government acquisition, operating-company continuity, purchase price and post-sale operation.
caveats: The owner-age proxy covers all US employer businesses and uses 2018 data.
Public sources emphasize notable transactions and omit quiet small-area transfers.
Sale of mountain assets or land does not always constitute transfer of an eligible operating firm.

q.bridge: Vail's reported pricing and revenue mix is a large-platform proxy for LMM ski areas, not a measurement of retained AI benefits. Its pass products generated 65% of lift revenue and 75% of visits in fiscal 2024, demonstrating advance fixed-price commitment and direct billing; Black Mountain's day tickets and season pass show the same mechanisms at a small operator. I infer high initial retention, then discount for pass renewal pricing, competition with conglomerate passes, customer discounts, service reinvestment and weather-related make-goods. Vail's brand and network likely overstate pricing power relative to independent areas.
q.rationale: There is no cost-plus contract forcing savings to customers. Advance passes, posted day tickets and capacity-limited mountain access let an operator retain implemented benefit until renewal or competitive repricing. Some benefit will be shared through lower prices, added service or reduced crowding, especially because independents compete against network passes and discretionary customer budgets.
q.change_evidence: A multi-operator panel linking automation savings to pass and day-ticket price changes, discount depth, renewals, visits, guest satisfaction, staffing, ancillary spend and EBITDA over at least three sales cycles.
caveats: No source directly measures retained AI benefit.
Capture varies with local capacity, snow quality, destination status and competing pass access.
Benefit reinvested in snowmaking or staffing improves resilience but is not retained as immediate cash.

d5.bridge: NSAA measures historical visits, not future constant-price, constant-quality demand for eligible LMM operators. National visits were 61.648 million in 2024/25, above 60.483 million in 2023/24 and below the 65.387 million record in 2022/23, showing strong but weather-sensitive demand. I start near the recent plateau, allow continued participation and pass convenience on the upside, and discount for discretionary spending, high prices and worsening snow reliability. NOAA's longer-horizon climate results point downward but vary sharply by elevation and region; applying them to only five years can overstate near-term loss, while recent high visitation can overstate the durable baseline.
d5.rationale: Recent national visitation is historically strong, supporting roughly stable five-year real quantity at the base. The same series shows large annual and regional variation, while climate evidence indicates later snow, earlier retreat and reduced season length at most resorts over time. Snowmaking and advance passes mitigate but do not remove weather, energy-cost and affordability risk.
d5.change_evidence: Resort-level NSAA visitation, ticket-price and operating-day data normalized for snowfall and snowmaking, combined with localized 2031 projections for wet-bulb snowmaking hours, water availability and season length for each target mountain.
caveats: Visits are realized utilization rather than a constant-price demand curve.
National totals mask major regional and elevation differences.
Climate projections cited are primarily for mid-century, so five-year impacts are inferred rather than directly measured.

o.rationale: Lift-served skiing requires an accountable entity to operate and inspect lifts, make and manage snow, groom terrain, patrol slopes, respond to incidents and control guest access. Software can replace purchases, communications and some decisions, but it cannot supply the physical mountain service or bear safety responsibility. Backcountry skiing and self-service digital channels can displace a small share of facility-mediated activity, while current ski-area demand overwhelmingly remains operator-required.
o.change_evidence: Consumer research quantifying substitution from lift-served skiing to backcountry access or indoor simulators, plus regulatory and insurance evidence on whether any mountain functions can operate without staffed accountable oversight.
caveats: This measures the need for an operator, not the number of employees required.
Automation can make a facility more self-service while leaving legal and safety accountability intact.
Facility closure from climate or economics belongs in demand, not operator substitution.

narrative.executive_view: Ski areas pair direct advance pricing with a service that remains physically operator-dependent, but the labor base is dominated by seasonal frontline work and the demand base is unusually exposed to weather and climate. AI can remove administrative friction and improve forecasting, yet it cannot replace lift, patrol, grooming, instruction and hospitality operations. The eligible independent universe appears meaningful but is not directly measured.
narrative.ai_impact: The strongest applications are customer questions, marketing, ticket and pass administration, rental and lesson reservations, demand forecasting, staffing plans, reporting, food ordering and snowmaking optimization. Integrated systems already let small areas manage products that would be impractical manually. Safety-critical field work remains human-led, and some important snowmaking automation is already in place rather than new opportunity.
narrative.value_capture: Day tickets and season passes are paid directly, often in advance, so there is no contractual pass-through of savings. Advance commitment also cushions weather variability. Independent resorts still compete against well-capitalized multi-mountain passes and may share benefits through discounts, service investment or more reliable snow rather than keeping all savings as margin.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry includes family independents, public and nonprofit areas, captive destination assets and concentrated multi-resort groups. Succession and strategic consolidation create transfers, as the Black Mountain case demonstrates, but lifts, permits, water, land arrangements, deferred capital and climate risk make transactions complex. No public source provides a qualifying transfer denominator.
narrative.demand_durability: National skier visits remain close to record levels, but yearly outcomes vary materially by snowfall and region. A near-flat five-year base is defensible because strong recent participation and pass adoption offset some weather exposure. Lower-elevation and warmer markets carry greater downside from shrinking snowmaking windows, while high-elevation western areas are more resilient but not immune.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The evidence is weakest on ski-specific occupation wages, representative AI adoption, eligible firm ownership and transfer frequency. Broad recreation data and Vail's scale may both overstate administrative opportunity for small areas. A bad target can be overwhelmed by lift capital, employee housing, energy and water costs, insurance, permits, wildfire, warming winters or reliance on one short peak season.
narrative.principal_driver: Direct advance pass revenue and unavoidable physical operator responsibility support retention of targeted digital-workflow benefits.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most labor is seasonal, physical and safety-critical, while weather and climate create unusually wide demand and investment risk.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 713900
sources.supports: Broader recreation occupation proxy, including office and administrative support at 10.37% of employment, food preparation and serving at 16.47%, and building and grounds maintenance at 9.91%.

sources.title: Vail Resorts Form 10-K for the Fiscal Year Ended July 31, 2024
sources.supports: Large-operator proxy: about 44,900 seasonal and 7,600 year-round employees; safety and staffing requirements; 65% of lift revenue and 75% of visits from passes; mountain revenue mix, labor cost, visitation and weather sensitivity.

sources.title: Ski Areas Adopt Technology to Transform Business
sources.supports: Black Mountain acquisition and independent-resort technology case: online ticketing, lessons and rentals; data-guided snowmaking; automated pass entitlements; self-service ordering; small-area pricing; and system implementation friction.

sources.title: Climate & Skiing
sources.supports: NOAA discussion of automated snowmaking alerts and controls, weather constraints on artificial snow, geographic variation and projected reductions in ski-season length.

sources.title: Number of Ski Areas in Operation: 2024/25 U.S. Ski Season
sources.supports: NSAA reports 492 operating ski areas across 37 states in 2024/25, defines a ski area by mechanized uphill access, and provides the historical operating-area series.

sources.title: Who Owns Which Mountain Resorts
sources.supports: NSAA ownership map for operators with at least three areas, including large groups, government ownership, joint partnerships and long-term operating agreements, supporting eligibility and consolidation caveats.

sources.title: Annual Business Survey Visualizations
sources.supports: Census states that over half of US business owners were age 55 and over in the 2019 ABS, used only as a cross-industry succession proxy.

sources.title: Historical U.S. Skier Visits by Region: 1978/79-2024/25
sources.supports: NSAA reports 61.648 million US skier visits in 2024/25, 60.483 million in 2023/24 and 65.387 million in 2022/23, with a long regional history showing demand level and volatility.
